items.title: Market data source governance
items.description: Market data source governance is the practice of cataloguing each data feed with its provider, coverage, update cadence, licensing terms, and known limitations. It lets downstream analytics know how much to trust a value and whether it may be redistributed. Governance also defines a single authoritative source per data point to avoid conflicting figures. In software, modelling sources as first-class records with metadata makes lineage and entitlement traceable from any derived signal.
items.applicability: Applies when integrating multiple market-data providers into an intelligence or forecasting platform.
items.example: Each price feed is registered with its provider, latency, licence scope, and a flag for whether it may be displayed externally, and signals record which source they used.
items.limitation: Analytical and forecast intelligence only; NOT financial or investment advice. Data licensing and redistribution rules are provider- and jurisdiction-specific and must be confirmed against current terms. No outcome is guaranteed.

items.title: Source reliability and freshness scoring
items.description: Reliability and freshness scoring attaches a quantified quality measure to each data point based on the trustworthiness of its source and how recently it was updated. Stale or low-reliability data can then be down-weighted or excluded from signals rather than silently corrupting them. Scores are typically derived from source reputation, historical accuracy, and the age of the value against its expected cadence. Surfacing these scores lets consumers understand the confidence behind any derived intelligence.
items.applicability: Applies to data-quality layers in market-intelligence platforms that blend feeds of varying timeliness and trust.
items.example: A quote updated within seconds from a primary feed scores high freshness and reliability, while an hour-old value from a secondary source is down-weighted in the blended signal.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. Scores are heuristics, not guarantees of accuracy. Past reliability does not ensure future accuracy. Confirm provider terms and suitability for your use.

items.title: News and event impact detection
items.description: Event impact detection links incoming news and scheduled events to the instruments they may affect and estimates a potential relevance or impact signal. It typically classifies events by type and entity, then associates them with watchlist items for downstream analysis. The output is an analytical signal of possible relevance, not a prediction of price direction. Systems should retain the originating event and the basis for any impact estimate so the signal can be explained and audited.
items.applicability: Applies to news-aware analytics in market-intelligence platforms that map events to instruments.
items.example: An earnings announcement is tagged to its issuer and surfaced as a high-relevance event for that instrument's watchers, with the source article linked.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice and NOT a prediction of price movement. Relevance is heuristic. No buy/sell recommendation. Past reactions do not guarantee future ones.

items.title: Macro indicators
items.description: Macro indicators are broad economic measures, such as growth, inflation, employment, and rate-related series, used to provide context for market analysis. They are usually released on schedules and can be revised, so analytics must handle versioned values rather than assuming finality. Macro context helps frame other signals but does not by itself indicate any action. In software, storing release dates, revisions, and the as-of view keeps historical analysis reproducible.
items.applicability: Applies to contextual analytics in market-intelligence platforms that incorporate economic data.
items.example: An inflation series is stored with its release and revision dates so a backtest can use only the value that was known at the time being analysed.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. Macro indicators provide context, not signals to act. Values are revised; past readings do not predict future results.

items.title: Company fundamentals
items.description: Company fundamentals are financial and operational metrics derived from reported statements, such as revenue, earnings, margins, and balance-sheet figures, used to characterise an issuer. They are periodic and subject to restatement, so analytics should track the reporting period and any later corrections. Fundamentals support comparison and screening but are descriptive inputs, not directives. Systems should preserve the as-reported figures and their source filing for transparency.
items.applicability: Applies to fundamental analytics and screening in market-intelligence platforms.
items.example: An issuer's quarterly revenue and margin are stored against the reporting period, letting a screen compare peers on consistent, point-in-time figures.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. Fundamentals are descriptive inputs, not recommendations. Figures may be restated; past results do not predict future performance.

items.title: Technical indicators (analytical only)
items.description: Technical indicators are calculations derived from price and volume history, such as moving averages or momentum measures, used to describe past and current market behaviour. They summarise patterns in historical data and can be inputs to analysis, but they do not foresee future prices. Implementations should compute them deterministically and document their parameters so results are reproducible. Presenting indicators as descriptive analytics, with no implied recommendation, keeps their role honest.
items.applicability: Applies to charting and analytical features in market-intelligence platforms.
items.example: A moving-average crossover is computed and displayed as a descriptive analytic on a chart, with the window lengths shown and no action implied.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice and NOT a buy/sell signal. Indicators describe history; they do not predict prices. Past performance does not predict future results.

items.title: Crypto on-chain and sentiment signal architecture
items.description: For digital assets, signal architecture often blends on-chain metrics, such as activity and flow measures derived from public ledgers, with off-chain sentiment from social and news sources. Each input has different reliability and latency, so the design should normalise, time-align, and quality-score them before combining. On-chain data is transparent but noisy, and sentiment is easily skewed, so both should be treated as weak signals to be weighted, not trusted blindly. Lineage from raw input to combined signal should be preserved for review.
items.applicability: Applies to digital-asset analytics in market-intelligence platforms that ingest on-chain and sentiment data.
items.example: A combined signal blends a quality-scored on-chain activity metric with a normalised sentiment score, recording each input's source and weight.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. Crypto signals are noisy and volatile; combination does not imply reliability. No buy/sell recommendation; past behaviour does not predict future results.

items.title: Sentiment scoring
items.description: Sentiment scoring quantifies the tone of text such as news or social posts into a numeric measure used as an analytical input. Because language is ambiguous and sources can be manipulated or unrepresentative, scores carry meaningful uncertainty and should be presented with confidence and source context. Aggregating sentiment across many items reduces but does not remove noise. Systems should retain the underlying items and the scoring method so a score can be inspected rather than taken on faith.
items.applicability: Applies to text-analytics components in market-intelligence platforms that derive sentiment signals.
items.example: A daily sentiment score for an instrument aggregates many articles, is shown with a confidence band, and links back to the contributing items.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. Sentiment is a noisy, manipulable signal, not a recommendation. No guaranteed outcome; past sentiment does not predict future results.
